content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 17, 1976

Clayton Roche
Deputy Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Ste 550
Sacramento CA 95814

76-08-201

Re: Opinion Request
CV 76/128 IL

Dear Mr. Roche:

You have requested our views on the opinion requested
by Francis Sarguis (No. 76-128). The following represents
the facts as we understand them and our view relative to
whether Mr. Sarguis must disqualify himself from participa-
tion in certain decisions of the Santa Barbara Metropolitan
Transit District.

With respect to the facts, we understand that Mr. Sarguis
recently was appointed to the Highway Commission. He also is
an attorney with his own private practice. One of his clients,
which has retained him for ten years, is the Santa Barbara
Metropolitan Transit District. The general duties which
Mr. Sarguis performs for the district are set forth in
Public Utilities Code Section 29533. He estimates that he
renders between eight and 15 hours of service per month
to the Transit District. Specifically, Mr. Sarguis has
asked whether his service on the Highway Commission constitutes
a conflict of interest which would require him to disqualify
himself from participating in the making of certain Transit
District decisions. We conclude that no such conflict exists.

Government Code Section 87100 provides:

"No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

Government Code Section 82048 states that a public official
means every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state
or local government agency. Government Code Section 82041

/31

Clayton Roche
August 17, 1976
Page Two

states that local government agency means a county, city or
district of any kind. In light of these definitions, if
Mr. Sarguis is a consultant, he is a public official subject
to the provisions of Government Code Section 87100.

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2) provides:

"Consultant" shall include any natural person who
provides, under contract, information, advice, recom-
mendation or counsel to a state or local government
agency, provided, however, that "consultant" shall not
include a person who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives at con-
clusions with respect to his or her rendition of
information, advice, recommendation or counsel
independent of the control and direction of the
agency or of any agency official, other than nor-
mal contract monitoring; and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect to
any agency decision beyond the rendition of in-
formation, advice, recommendation or counsel.

Public Utilities Code Section 95533 sets forth the
duties of the Transit District attorney. It is not clear
from a reading of this statute whether the attorney's author-
ity goes beyond the rendition of information, advice, recom-
mendation or counsel. However, the fact that Mr. Sarguis
has served the District for more than ten years suggests
that his duties may have expanded beyond the narrowest pos-
sible reading of the statute, and that he may be a "consultant."

Assuming arguendo that Mr. Sarguis is a consultant and
that he does make or participate in the making of governmental
decisions by the Transit District, we nevertheless have
concluded that there are no financial interests which foresee-
ably could be materially affected by any such decisions.
The term "material financial interest" is defined in Govern-
ment Code Section 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18702.
These sections refer to financial interests such as investments,
interests in real property or sources of income. Clearly
the Highway Commission does not constitute an investment or
an interest in real property for Mr. Sarguis. Moreover, it
is not a source of income in light of Government Code Section
87101(b)(2), which expressly excludes from income salary,
reimbursement for expenses and per diem received from a
state or local governmental agency. Accordingly, service on
the Highway Commission does not present a forseeable con-
flict of interest for Mr. Sarguis in his capacity as a public
official of the Transit District. Therefore, he need not
disqualify himself from making or participating in the making

Clayton Roche
August 17, 1976
Page Three


of any governmental decision of the Transit District by
virtue of his service on the Highway Commission.

We wish to emphasize that we intimate no decision
relative to whether Mr. Sarguis' service as a consultant
to the Transit District poses a conflict of interest for
him in his capacity as a public official with the Highway
Commission. If you find that Mr. Sarguis also wishes to
receive an opinion on this question, we would appreciate
the opportunity to present our views.

Since Mr. Sarguis only requests a letter opinion, we
assume that any opinion rendered by your office will be
unpublished. If this is not the case, we would appreciate
notice of the form your opinion will take. If you have any
further questions, please contact me or Ted Prim, an attorney
with the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

TP:MH:pvp

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                           EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

CALIFORNIA HIGHWAY COMMISSION
1120 N STREET, P.O. BOX 1159
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95805


FRANCIS SARGUIS, Member
Post Office Box 816
Santa Barbara, CA 93102


                                        June 22, 1976


Deputy Clayton Roche
Office of Attorney General
Department of Justice
Government Law Section
350 McAllister Street, Rm. 6000
San Francisco, CA  94102

Re:  Opinion Request
     Your No. CV 76/128 IL

Dear Mr. Roche:

     I am advised by Mr. Skjele of the Sacramento Office of the Attorney
General that my opinion request relating to the question of conflict
of interest has been referred to you for research and writing.

     I know how loaded your office must be with opinion requests and other
tasks.  Bearing this in mind, I would still very much appreciate it
if the opinion were issued as soon as possible, as it would greatly
facilitate my work with the MTD.

     I would also like to add the following clarification:  While my
letter of May 26 addressed itself to "conflict of interest," I believe
that the comments I made in that letter also raise by implication the
issue of "compatibility of offices."  Therefore, it would be appre-
ciated if you would specifically deal with both questions, and not
just one.

Sincerely,


Francis Sarguis

FS:cll

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                     EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

CALIFORNIA HIGHWAY COMMISSION
1120 N STREET, P.O. BOX 1139
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95805

Francis Sarguis, Commission Member
P.O. Box 816
Santa Barbara, CA  93102
                                                OPINION REQUEST
                                                CV 76/128 IL

                    May 26, 1976


Office of Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, CA  95814

To Which It May Concern:  Dear Mr. Younger:

    Nearly two months ago, I was appointed by Governor Brown to the
California Highway Commission.

    I am an attorney by occupation, sole practitioner, with a general
practice.  Among my various clients, I have one which is a public
agency, to wit, the Santa Barbara Metropolitan Transit District
(SBMTD).

    I have rendered legal services to the SBMTD on an hourly basis, ever
since the SBMTD was created some ten years ago.  The District operates
pursuant to the enabling legislation found under Public Utilities Code
§95900 et seq, and the nature of my legal services to the Board is
more particularly described in §95533.  I estimate that I render between
8 and 15 hours of service per month to the SBMTD.  My other legal work
ranges over many areas, such as domestic relations, probate law, etc.

    As a result of my appointment to the State Highway Commission, one of
the SBMTD Board members has asked whether my Commission appointment
poses any conflict of interest problem with my legal work for the
SBMTD.  I personally considered this matter prior to receiving the
appointment and came to a negative conclusion.  The same question was
also discussed with Secretary Donald Burns of the Business and Trans-
portation Agency at the time he considered my appointment.  He likewise
came up with a negative conclusion.  Lastly, since the matter was
brought up by one of our SBMTD Board members, I have also raised this
question with Kingsley Hoegstedt, who is legal counsel for the Highway
Commission.  His conclusion was likewise negative.

    As Mr. Hoegstedt points out, I am not occupying an office with the
SBMTD; in a manner of speaking, I am something akin to an "employee."

                                                        JUN 8 '76

Office of Attorney General
May 25, 1976
Page two


Thus, although I hold no "office" at the SBMTD, the Board would
nevertheless like confirmation that no conflict exists. Accordingly,
I wish to solicit from you a letter-opinion on this question. If I
can be of any assistance in providing any other information which
you deem pertinent to the inquiry, I am of course prepared to assist
in any way I can.

Sincerely,

Francis Sarguis

sections.conclusion: We conclude that no such conflict of interest arises from Mr. Sarguis' service on the Highway Commission with respect to his legal work for the Transit District. No public office or local government position is incompatible. If Mr. Sarguis is a consultant under the law, he is a public official subject to Government Code Section 87100 provisions, but there are no financial interests which would be materially affected by Transit District decisions. Mr. Sarguis' service as a consultant does not constitute a managerial decision of the Transit District by virtue of his Highway Commission service.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Mr. Sarguis' concurrent service as legal counsel to the Santa Barbara Metropolitan Transit District while serving on the California Highway Commission create a conflict of interest or compatibility of offices problem under Government Code Section 87100?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Sarguis' concurrent service as legal counsel to the Transit District and member of the Highway Commission does not create a conflict of interest, as no financial interests of Mr. Sarguis would be materially affected by Transit District decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality issues throughout document affecting readability. Document includes both the incoming request letters (May 26 and June 22, 1976) and FPPC's response (August 17, 1976). The conclusion section is fragmented due to OCR errors but main legal conclusions are extractable. The FPPC notes it is not addressing whether Sarguis' Transit District consulting poses a conflict for his Highway Commission role—only the reverse.

Thus, although I hold no "office" at the SBMTD, the Board would

in any way I can.

CONCLUSION: We conclude that no such conflict of interest arises from Mr. Sarguis' service on the Highway Commission with respect to his legal work for the Transit District. No public office or local government position is incompatible. If Mr. Sarguis is a consultant under the law, he is a public official subject to Government Code Section 87100 provisions, but there are no financial interests which would be materially affected by Transit District decisions. Mr. Sarguis' service as a consultant does not constitute a managerial decision of the Transit District by virtue of his Highway Commission service.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to an opinion request from Francis Sarguis regarding whether his service as legal counsel to the Santa Barbara Metropolitan Transit District is compatible with his appointment to the California Highway Commission. The FPPC advises that no conflict of interest exists.
